name: rye
shortDescription: A hardy sack of rye grain, darker and earthier than wheat, favored by bakers who want bread with bite and by villages that cannot rely on gentle soil. It keeps well in dry stores and gives poor taverns something honest to serve when finer flour runs short.
flavorText: “Rye grows where polite crops make excuses.”

name: oats
shortDescription: A plain measure of oats used for porridge, horse feed, travel cakes, and the sort of practical cooking that keeps workers moving. They rarely impress nobles, but stablemasters, innkeepers, and caravan cooks all know how quickly a town becomes grateful for them.
flavorText: “Not glamorous, not rare, and never left unsold by winter.”

name: quinoa
shortDescription: A small-grained crop prized by hill traders, temple cooks, and health-minded nobles who enjoy pretending their supper is a discipline. It is light, filling, and fetches better prices in cities where foreign staples are treated as sophistication.
flavorText: “Tiny grains, large claims.”

name: rice stalk
shortDescription: A bundled rice stalk cut before threshing, still carrying the pale rustle of wet fields and careful harvest hands. Farmers buy it as feed, bedding, or proof of crop quality, while millers judge the season by how the stalk bends in their grip.
flavorText: “Every grain begins as a whisper in the marsh.”

name: oats stalk
shortDescription: A dry bundle of oat stalks tied for storage, useful as fodder, bedding, packing straw, or cheap thatch in a hurry. It smells of barns and honest work, the kind of trade good that moves by wagonload rather than by applause.
flavorText: “A stable can measure wealth by how much clean straw it wastes.”

name: barley stalk
shortDescription: A sheaf of barley stalks with stiff stems and heavy heads, valued by brewers, millers, and livestock owners before the grain is fully separated. The bundle is bulky but dependable, carrying both feed and future ale in one rough-handed package.
flavorText: “Some merchants see straw. Brewers see tomorrow.”

name: rye stalk
shortDescription: A tall rye stalk bundle with dark seed heads and wiry stems, cut from fields that often survive where softer grains fail. It is useful for thatch, bedding, feed, and seed inspection, especially in frontier markets where hardiness matters more than beauty.
flavorText: “Rye stands in the wind like it has already been disappointed.”

name: wheat stalk
shortDescription: A golden bundle of wheat stalks, bright enough to decorate harvest doors and practical enough to feed mills, beasts, or ovens once properly worked. Its value rises quickly near hungry cities, where even a rough sheaf can look like a promise.
flavorText: “The crown of the field is still straw until someone threshes it.”

name: quinoa stalk
shortDescription: A colorful quinoa stalk gathered from high, stubborn fields, still showing the clustered heads that make buyers lean closer. It is light compared with common grain sheaves, but specialty cooks and seed traders often pay extra for clean, healthy bundles.
flavorText: “Mountain crops travel light because the road home was never kind.”

name: chair with cushion
shortDescription: A sturdy wooden chair fitted with a padded cushion, comfortable enough for counting ledgers, hearing petitions, or surviving a long meal beside dull company. It is not royal furniture, but it signals a household that has moved beyond benches and patience.
flavorText: “A cushion is how a chair asks to be respected.”

name: chair with cushion and arms
shortDescription: A broad chair with padded seat, carved arms, and enough presence to claim a favored corner of any room. Magistrates, wealthy innkeepers, and older merchants prize it because comfort becomes more persuasive when it has somewhere to rest its elbows.
flavorText: “The arms are not for resting. They are for looking settled.”

name: damaged throne
shortDescription: A battered throne with cracked ornament, scuffed legs, and the stubborn dignity of furniture that once heard orders. Collectors may call it historical, rebels may call it satisfying, and practical buyers may simply see expensive wood in need of repair.
flavorText: “Nothing ages faster than authority with a broken leg.”

name: desk
shortDescription: A serviceable writing desk with drawers for ink, contracts, loose coins, and secrets too small for a strongbox. Merchants favor a flat surface that can hold both accounts and accusations, especially when the wood is sturdy enough for angry fists.
flavorText: “Most fortunes begin with a table. The dangerous ones begin with a desk.”

name: worn desk
shortDescription: An old desk rubbed smooth by years of elbows, spilled ink, and desperate arithmetic. Its corners are nicked and its drawers complain, but the piece still has enough use left for a clerk, apothecary, or anyone rebuilding a business from poor paper.
flavorText: “The wood remembers every total that refused to balance.”

name: well crafted table
shortDescription: A carefully built table with balanced legs, clean joinery, and a surface that invites maps, meals, or negotiations. It is the kind of household piece that costs more than rough planks because it does not wobble when the conversation does.
flavorText: “A good table lets enemies sit close without spilling the wine.”

name: well crafted small table
shortDescription: A compact table made with care, easy to place beside a bed, chair, shrine, or shop counter. It will not dominate a room, but its neat finish makes it useful for homes where every object must earn its space politely.
flavorText: “Small furniture survives by being exactly where it is needed.”

name: large rough table
shortDescription: A heavy rough table built for work rather than admiration, with thick boards, blunt edges, and the patience to survive knives, mugs, tools, and elbows. Taverns, workshops, and farmhouses value it because refinement breaks sooner than bulk.
flavorText: “It has held arguments, stew, and probably a sleeping guard.”

name: large dresser
shortDescription: A large dresser with deep drawers and enough presence to anchor a bedchamber, storeroom, or merchant guesthouse. Its worth lies in storage, not elegance, though a polished face can still make linens and spare clothes look wealthier than they are.
flavorText: “A dresser is a chest that learned manners.”

name: large worn dresser
shortDescription: A big dresser that has seen long service, with tired pulls, scratched panels, and drawers that know several forms of complaint. It remains useful despite its scars, especially for buyers who need storage more urgently than they need appearances.
flavorText: “Old drawers keep old habits.”

name: elegant low table
shortDescription: A low table with graceful lines, polished finish, and enough refinement for tea rooms, private chambers, or quiet negotiation spaces. It is less suited to feasting than to display, placing a bowl, lamp, or delicate bargain exactly where guests must notice it.
flavorText: “Low tables encourage calm voices and expensive mistakes.”

name: long dresser
shortDescription: A long dresser built to spread storage across a wall, its drawers suited for folded cloth, household goods, or the inventory of someone who refuses to call clutter a problem. Inns and affluent homes like it because it looks orderly even when full.
flavorText: “Order is easier to admire when hidden behind wood.”

name: long worn dresser
shortDescription: A stretched, aging dresser with a scuffed top and drawers softened by too many years of use. It lacks elegance, but it offers generous storage at a kinder price, making it welcome in boarding houses, workshops, and repaired estates.
flavorText: “Long enough to hold a wardrobe. Old enough to judge it.”

name: massive elegant table
shortDescription: A grand table made for halls, councils, feasts, and owners who want furniture to announce their importance before they do. Its size makes transport miserable, but its polish and craftsmanship can transform a room into a place where decisions feel official.
flavorText: “Some tables are bought by the foot. This one is bought by ambition.”

name: well crafted low table
shortDescription: A low table built with careful joinery and a clean, practical surface, suitable for sitting rooms, floor cushions, merchant displays, or polite hospitality. It is understated but dependable, the sort of piece that quietly makes cheap rooms feel considered.
flavorText: “Good craft does not need height to be noticed.”

name: metal bed
shortDescription: A sturdy metal bed frame with cold rails, firm joints, and a reputation for surviving moves, guests, and suspiciously active nights. It is heavier than wooden frames but less vulnerable to pests, damp, and careless lodgers with boots.
flavorText: “Comfort is negotiable. Durability is not.”

name: worn nightstand
shortDescription: A small worn nightstand with a scratched top, sticky drawer, and just enough dignity left to hold a candle, cup, book, or emergency dagger. Buyers rarely brag about owning one, but almost everyone discovers they need it after dark.
flavorText: “The humblest furniture hears the most midnight confessions.”

name: purple bed
shortDescription: A bed dressed in purple cloth and decorative trim, far showier than a plain frame and often purchased by those who want sleep to look expensive. It suits guest rooms, performers, minor nobles, and anyone confusing color with authority.
flavorText: “Purple is what wood wears when it wants a title.”

name: narrow purple bed
shortDescription: A slim bed with purple dressing, made for tight rooms that still wish to pretend they are grand. It is practical in footprint and theatrical in color, a compromise popular with inns trying to sell romance by the night.
flavorText: “Small rooms dream bigger when draped correctly.”

name: red double bed
shortDescription: A double bed with bold red fabric, broad enough for comfort and vivid enough to dominate a chamber. It appeals to newlyweds, prosperous inns, and dramatic owners who believe a bedroom should enter the room before they do.
flavorText: “A red bed never claims to be modest.”

name: red bed
shortDescription: A single red bed with bright cloth, firm frame, and a hint of theatrical confidence. It is less costly than a grand double but still catches the eye, making plain rooms feel warm, lively, and perhaps a little overconfident.
flavorText: “Sleep is easier to sell when it has color.”

name: royal bed
shortDescription: A lavish royal bed built for deep cushions, rich hangings, and the kind of comfort that requires servants to maintain. Its price includes craftsmanship, transport trouble, and the dangerous idea that anyone sleeping in it deserves obedience.
flavorText: “Kings rise from beds like this. So do people pretending.”

name: shelf
shortDescription: A plain shelf for books, jars, tools, folded goods, or whatever a household is tired of stepping around. It is simple furniture, but a shopkeeper knows that vertical storage can turn a cramped corner into profit.
flavorText: “A shelf is a floor that learned to climb.”

name: worn shelf
shortDescription: A scuffed shelf with old peg marks, uneven edges, and enough strength left to hold jars, ledgers, or rolled cloth. It lacks elegance but offers cheap order, which is often the first luxury a struggling shop can afford.
flavorText: “It may sag, but it still lifts things above the dust.”

name: single bed
shortDescription: A plain single bed made for one sleeper, one rented room, or one tired guard who claims he only closed his eyes briefly. It is not luxurious, but it is easy to place, easy to sell, and always needed somewhere.
flavorText: “Solitude costs less in timber.”

name: small broken table
shortDescription: A small table with a cracked top, weak joint, or leg that has learned dishonesty. It is poor furniture as sold, but useful for repairers, students, cheap lodgings, or anyone who can forgive a wobble in exchange for a bargain.
flavorText: “Every broken table believes it has one more supper left.”

name: small rough dresser
shortDescription: A compact rough dresser built from plain boards, useful where space and coin are both in short supply. Its finish is simple, its drawers stubborn, and its value obvious to anyone who has ever tried living out of a sack.
flavorText: “A humble drawer can rescue a room from chaos.”

name: small rough table
shortDescription: A small rough table with blunt construction and little ornament, ready for workshops, kitchens, rented rooms, or market stalls. It is not beautiful, but it offers a flat place to cut, count, mend, eat, or argue.
flavorText: “Four legs and no pretension: civilization begins there.”

name: small elegant table
shortDescription: A refined small table with polished lines, useful as a side piece, display stand, or polite resting place for cups and correspondence. Its charm lies in restraint, allowing a room to look wealthy without needing to shout.
flavorText: “Elegance is often just usefulness with cleaner edges.”

name: long elegant table
shortDescription: A long elegant table suited for formal meals, careful displays, or negotiations where presentation matters nearly as much as the terms. The surface is generous, the shape graceful, and the buyer usually someone expecting guests to notice both.
flavorText: “A long table gives silence more room to work.”

name: small rack
shortDescription: A small rack for hanging tools, cups, cloth, weapons, or whatever must be found quickly and not buried in a chest. It is modest household infrastructure, the kind of thing nobody praises until it is missing.
flavorText: “Hooks are humble until the floor disappears under clutter.”

name: sofa with cushion
shortDescription: A cushioned sofa built for lingering, gossip, tired bones, and visitors who should be kept comfortable but not necessarily invited to stay forever. It takes room, but in a parlor it turns empty space into social strategy.
flavorText: “A sofa is a bench that learned persuasion.”

name: stool with cushion
shortDescription: A compact stool topped with a cushion, useful for musicians, shop counters, dressing rooms, or any corner where comfort must fit into a small footprint. It is light enough to move and soft enough to justify the extra coin.
flavorText: “A little padding can make a long wait seem civilized.”

name: short stool
shortDescription: A low stool of simple construction, ready for hearths, workbenches, milking sheds, children, tired feet, or anyone who needs to sit without making a ceremony of it. Its value is plain, portable, and hard to argue with.
flavorText: “The shortest seat often does the most work.”

name: throne with cushion
shortDescription: A throne fitted with a cushion, meant to soften the body while hardening the impression of authority. It is less austere than bare carved wood and far more comfortable for anyone planning to sit in judgment longer than justice requires.
flavorText: “Power dislikes splinters.”

name: wooden bed
shortDescription: A dependable wooden bed frame built from honest timber, easier to warm than metal and easier to repair than luxury furniture. It suits homes, inns, barracks, and spare rooms where plain comfort matters more than spectacle.
flavorText: “Good sleep rarely asks what the carvings cost.”

name: well crafted wooden chair
shortDescription: A carefully joined wooden chair with smooth edges, steady legs, and enough craft to survive daily use without sounding like a complaint. It is a reliable purchase for dining rooms, offices, guild halls, and respectable waiting spaces.
flavorText: “The best chairs are noticed only when replaced by bad ones.”

name: fancy wooden chair
shortDescription: A decorative wooden chair with carved details and a posture more formal than friendly. It suits parlors, audience rooms, and merchants who want visitors to see taste, even if the seat itself encourages everyone to finish business quickly.
flavorText: “Fancy chairs are designed for being seen before being sat upon.”

name: fence length
shortDescription: A length of simple fencing ready to mark fields, pens, gardens, or disputed boundaries before arguments become lawsuits. It is ordinary timber shaped into one of civilization’s most useful declarations: this side is mine, that side is not.
flavorText: “The first law of property is a line people can trip over.”

name: iron fence length
shortDescription: A sturdy iron fence length, heavier and costlier than wood but far harder to ignore, break, or burn. Estates, temples, prisons, and rich gardens value it wherever boundary must become warning as much as decoration.
flavorText: “Iron does not ask politely where the edge is.”

name: strong fence length
shortDescription: A reinforced fence length built for durability, livestock pressure, bad weather, and neighbors who test limits with wagons. It is not elegant, but it holds its line well enough to save coin, beasts, and temper over many seasons.
flavorText: “A weak fence invites debate. A strong one ends it.”
